claims.quote: They have been forced to witness the gradual melting away of values for many montas, until, in the opinion of many, the bottom was close at hand.
claims.note: Reported market call that the decline is near its end. Included as a forecast of market direction, not a description of the past week.

claims.quote: Some unfavorable news is expected.
claims.note: Terse but a genuine forward claim about the smelting/refining group.

claims.quote: It is stated that but few orders are being booked, and that a shutdown of many of the corporation's mille is only a question of a few weeks cr months at the longest.
claims.note: Dated directional call on US Steel mill shutdowns; industrial-activity claim, not a stock tip.

claims.quote: Banks have passed through the week with a fairly steady money market, but the crop movement is heavy, and there is little chance for a change toward easier money in the near future.
claims.note: Second clause is a committed forecast that money stays tight.

claims.quote: The strongest possible hope of a rally comes in the oversold condition of the market, and the fact that the shorts would turn quickly if the news was more favorahie.
claims.note: Commits to a direction (rally) despite the conditional tail, so not excluded as a bare conditional.

excluded_notes: 'conservative bankers and others have been predicting that ... there would be a return of prosperity' -- EXCLUDED. The sentence appears under the headline 'Prophecies Gone Wrong' and its own assertion is that the forecasters were WRONG ('showing how the prophets were mistaken'). The frame is retrospective. This is the single most dangerous false positive on the page: an extractor that takes it records a bullish October-1907 forecast that would then be scored against the Panic, manufacturing exactly the 'nobody saw it coming' signal the project is trying to measure honestly.

'In any event, speculation is to be discouraged, while those who have ... and cash enough to buy securitic {ran not go far. wrong In taking on= gh grade rails at present prices.' -- EXCLUDED under the unreconstructable-OCR rule; the direction is guessable but the span is too broken to code confidently.
'it would take something new in the way of depressing influences to turn tha tide' -- EXCLUDED as a bare conditional with no committed direction.
'Admittedly the corporation is fixed to stand an era of depression' -- EXCLUDED: a present-tense claim about one company's balance sheet, not a forecast of conditions.
London market paragraph ('Anticipation of a better bank statement ... caused a slight improvement') -- EXCLUDED: describes what already happened in the session.
ZERO-PREDICTION PAGE, and a deliberate precision test. This is a crisis-window page (four months after the Panic of 1907) whose economic content is entirely retrospective or promotional.

Meat-market advertisement: 'THE PRICE OT WHICH WILL CAUSE TODAY'S TROUBLES TO LESSEN ... THE EFFECT OF WHICH WILL CAUSE THE FUTURE TO LOOK BRIQHTER' -- EXCLUDED as advertising. Note it is future-tense, economic-sounding, and contains 'WILL' twice: precisely the shape a keyword extractor mistakes for a forecast.
'BANK OF BISBEE HAS EXCELLENT REPORT ... it also speaks in loud tones for the prosperity of Bisbee' -- EXCLUDED: present-tense claim about a published balance sheet.
'No doubt some of these drafts have been lost or destroyed and will never be cashed' -- EXCLUDED: forward-looking, but about the redemption of specific drafts, not about economic conditions.
'there Is little probability of the disease speadlng at this time of ths year' -- EXCLUDED: non-economic (typhoid).
Royal Hotel cafe opening ('What promises to be one of tne most attractive ... cafes ... will tocay throw its doors open') -- EXCLUDED: a business announcement, not a forecast of conditions.

claims.quote: Baggs will be the chief business point of the valley and its citizens expect it to become a rival of even Rawlins and Cheyenne.
claims.note: Local rather than national, but a falsifiable forward claim about business conditions, which the protocol does not restrict by geographic scope.

excluded_notes: 'Then followed a brief period of business depression which gave the employers an opportunity to deal with their employees in an independent manner' -- EXCLUDED: history of 19th-century English unionism, retrospective.
'irrigation projects are being pushed and railroad will traverse it next summer' -- EXCLUDED: an infrastructure-project announcement, not a claim about economic conditions.

MUTUAL PROTECTIVE LEAGUE insurance advertisement ('shows it CAN pay all future obligation 100 per cent on the dollar ... How do you know they won't bust to-morrow?') -- EXCLUDED as advertising, again future-tense and financial in vocabulary.
'the rapidity of its future growth depends entirely upon the willingness of men to be taught' -- EXCLUDED: conditional, and about a tax movement rather than economic conditions.
'the chief charm about public office holding is the anticipation the reality is a bit different' -- EXCLUDED as aphorism.

claims.quote: BIG CHICAGO BANKER SAYS THE NEW MONEY LAW WILL HELP BUSINESS QUICKLY
claims.note: Headline that itself states the forecast; protocol counts headlines when they assert the prediction.

claims.quote: The new currency measure is bound to be a success, because it was drawn fairly and in the open,
claims.note: 'bound to be' is about as assertive as the period gets.

claims.quote: It's passage should bring in a Christmas spirit of optimism to replace a feeling of depression that was based on nothing at all.
claims.note: 'should' hedges; also a reassurance claim (the depression 'was based on nothing at all').

claims.quote: It will take at least three months to put the new system at work, but business will not wait the complete organization of the new federal reserve board and of the regional reserve banks.
claims.note: Carries an explicit timeframe, which is rare enough to matter for the specificity measure.

claims.quote: In my opinion there will be a response within the near future that will mean increased business activity.
claims.note: 'In my opinion' hedges an otherwise assertive 'there will be'.

claims.quote: The ultimate effect of the new system will be good.
claims.note: 'ultimate' puts the horizon beyond any codeable window.

claims.quote: The Owen-Glass bill, even its most bitter enemies admit, creates a machinery to avert panics.
claims.note: Span is split across a column break in the OCR ('...enemies ad' appears far above 'mit, creates a machinery to avert' + 'panics'). Reconstructable with confidence, so included; direction is forward (future panics averted) despite the present-tense verb.

claims.quote: Banks will not have to be quite so conservative; there will be a moderate expansion of credit, and within a short time business houses whose stocks are now nearly depleted, will become active" buyers.
claims.note: Three chained forward claims in one sentence, all the same direction and topic, so one record per the span rule.

excluded_notes: 'Much of the success of the new system will depend upon the character of the men on the reserve board.' -- EXCLUDED: conditional with no committed direction.
'But the country knows that the president will appoint only high-class mer[n]' -- EXCLUDED: a forecast about appointments, not economic conditions.
'Hulbert charged that much of the opposition to the new currency law ... was insincere' -- EXCLUDED: retrospective accusation.
'There'll be some awful scenes around the crowded counters, if they do.' -- EXCLUDED: non-economic joke about Christmas shoppers.
Aviation and Beachey material -- non-economic.

publisher: the richmond palladium and sun-telegram (richmond, ind.) 1907-1939

claims.quote: Encourag ing reports have been received from the states of Sonora and Sinloa, where the return of prosperity is noted by the announcement that these estates expect to export 15,000 carloads of agricultural products to the United States within the next few months.
claims.note: Forward claim with an explicit quantity and window. SCOPE CAVEAT: the subject is Mexican agricultural output, not the US economy. It satisfies the extraction rule (a falsifiable forward economic claim) but a downstream scorer would need a scope field to avoid grading it against US series -- the current schema has no such field.

claims.quote: It is said that the state of Sinloa will have at least 2,000 tons or refined sugar available for export.
claims.note: Same scope caveat as above.

excluded_notes: 'the cattle rais ers are getting so little of it that they are going into bankruptcy' -- EXCLUDED: present continuous, describes what is already happening.
'sheep and lambs are so low that there is nothing much be side a fond recollection left for the farmer' -- EXCLUDED: present-tense description of the 1920 farm collapse.

'Realizing that many working men and women of Richmond are out of employment ... the Rapp's Cut Price Co. has met the situation with their Big Blue Pencil Sale' -- EXCLUDED: explicitly marked 'Advertisement'. Another economic-vocabulary ad trap (it names unemployment directly).
'if the assessment of an Excess profits tax is upheld by the courts the premium is again taxed' -- EXCLUDED: conditional, and about tax incidence rather than economic conditions.
'the result of their efforts will come before a called meeting of the association, at an early date' -- EXCLUDED: an organizational schedule, not an economic forecast.

publisher: the brookings register (brookings, brookings county, s.d.) 1903-1971
excluded_notes: ZERO-PREDICTION PAGE, and the single most diagnostic page in the sample. It is a small-town social-notes page from the depth of the 1920-21 depression, and it contains NO economic forecast of any kind.

Farmers National Bank advertisement: '1921. The dawn of better days-thebeginning of better times for all people. May the New Year bring you sunshine and happiness, a return of prosperity.' -- EXCLUDED as advertising, and the second sentence is a WISH ('May the New Year bring you...'), not an assertion. CRITICAL: this ad contains the literal string 'return of prosperity', which is one of the scraper's exact search phrases and the source of 300 claims in the v1 corpus. New Year advertising in December/January is saturated with 'prosperity' boilerplate. A phrase search run over a December window will preferentially retrieve pages like this one, and a future-tense regex will read the ad copy as a bullish forecast. This is a plausible mechanism for systematic optimism bias in the v1 corpus that is independent of what newspapers actually predicted.
'Wishing You a Prosperous 1921' (Grimm's) and 'wish you prosperity and happiness for the New Year' (A. E. Anderson, Druggist) -- EXCLUDED: the same seasonal-greeting pattern, twice more on one page.

'Expensive dairy barns and equipment will prob ably make dairying more ini'Testing, and increase somewhat the total pro duction' -- EXCLUDED: general husbandry advice, not a dated forecast of conditions.

'There is but little doubt that the nomination will be confirmed' -- EXCLUDED: non-economic, and reprinted from a 'Twenty-five Years Ago' column, so its information time is 1895, not 1920. A date-stamped extractor would misattribute it.
'They will have a plenty before long' (Slats' Diary) -- EXCLUDED: a joke about wedding gifts.

claims.quote: With the return of prosperity. In- dustries will clamor for women work ers. In the opinion of Miss Mary E. McDowell of the University of 'hirago Settlement house, who is chairman of the women in industry I'ornmitteo of the National league of Women Voters.
claims.note: A GENUINE hit for the search phrase 'return of prosperity' -- worth contrasting with page 5, where the same phrase appeared only in a bank advertisement.

claims.quote: Kvery paper dealing house In the country is advising its patrons to say to bankers and business men that there will bo no use for holding back or curtailing the use of printing on ac count of price, as there is no possi bility of reducing prices on any of the cards, papers and materials used by printers for months to come, that in fact there will bo some advances over present prices.
claims.note: price_direction=up. Coded 'worsen' per the protocol's rule to read prices through the sentence's own framing: printed in the 1920-21 deflation, it frames continued high and rising input prices as the absence of relief, not as recovery.

excluded_notes: 'I belie-vo that when figures of the 192D census are available, they will show more than 1?,000,000 women in gainful occupation.' -- EXCLUDED, and a close call. It is a falsifiable forward-dated statement, but what it forecasts is the content of a data release describing a PAST period, not future economic conditions. Expect extractors to disagree with the gold here.

January Clearance Sale advertisement: 'You will still have plenty ot wear out ot them this season and you will not buy them for less next year.' -- EXCLUDED as advertising. Note it is a genuine future-tense PRICE claim; ad copy routinely makes price forecasts, which is why the ad exclusion has to be applied before the topic test, not after.
'Sufficient funds are still available for taking care of the necessary repair work next spring' -- EXCLUDED: a public-works schedule.
'While tho purchase price would be cheap it would require heavy expense for additions and improvements' -- EXCLUDED: conditional, single-company.

'United States Smelting and Refining company declared a quarterly dividend of 60 cents a share. Heretofore $1.50 has been tho quarterly dividend.' -- EXCLUDED: a completed corporate action. Economically informative (a 60% dividend cut in the 1920-21 slump) but not a forecast.
'in tho past year of mining depression produced oro to tho amount of MIGHT MJLJjION DOLLARS' -- EXCLUDED: retrospective.

claims.quote: Confidence that business conditions from now on v;l shew a gradual improvement vvae; expressed in a business review u-.-.tied by the United States Chamber f Commerce
claims.note: Speaker named later in the paragraph as the review's author; the protocol allows clearly-implied attribution.

claims.quote: The most convincing evidence that a re on the high road to recovery i is found in the universal report from tciy section of the country of the j stcnclMy growing sentiment that business will be better in the spring.
claims.note: 'better in the spring' from a February paper is a real, datable horizon -- unusually specific for this corpus.

claims.quote: Th? review predicted there would net be a sudden return to prosperity j but a 'slow and natural working out j c f these processes of liquidation I which are now in full operation.'
claims.note: DIRECTION TRAP. The span contains the string 'return to prosperity' but NEGATES it ('there would not be a sudden return'). The committed direction is still improvement, just gradual -- so 'improve', hedged. Any extractor that keys on the phrase rather than the negation will code this wrongly, and it is exactly the failure mode a keyword pipeline cannot see.

excluded_notes: Headline 'BUSINESS SLOWLY GROWING BETTER' -- EXCLUDED, narrowly. It asserts a present trend ('growing'), not a future state. The protocol counts headlines only when the headline itself states a forecast; this one states a current condition and sits above a story that does forecast. A defensible opposite call, and flagged as such.

'It will be impossible under the present wasteful appropriation system ... to bring about the economy and efficiency in public business' -- EXCLUDED: advocacy for the budget bill, about government administration rather than economic conditions.
'This promises to be the greatest trade carnival ever held in this section of the state' (Dollar Day) -- EXCLUDED as advertising.
Apartments-and-babies editorial ('why wouldn't manv parents be willing and glad to get such accommodatios') -- EXCLUDED: speculative social commentary, no falsifiable claim about conditions.
'A new date for the sale will be announced later' -- EXCLUDED: an announcement about an announcement.
Pepto-Mangan and Neolin advertisements -- EXCLUDED.

claims.quote: He tvarne.u that "when business revives, when hun dreds of millions of dollars must rlow out of bank reserves into hand to hand circulations, and when com mercial borrowing increases with re viving trade, we shall have to pull this money out of the securities mar ket again, a process which will not i.elp along the business revival.
claims.note: Coded 'improve' because the sentence PRESUPPOSES revival ('when business revives', not 'if'); the warning is about the mechanics of the exit, not about whether recovery comes. A defensible alternative coding is 'unclear'. Printed June 1930, i.e. an economist at Chase confidently assuming recovery nine months into the Depression -- substantively the kind of claim this project exists to score.

claims.quote: Mayor Quigley said there are 1.6'i'i applicants for city work now and: as many more who are near the end i of their small savings who will soon [be] coming to city ... aid
claims.note: SPAN CAVEAT: the sentence is broken across interleaved columns in the OCR; reconstructed conservatively. RECALL CASE: a real forward claim about relief demand, buried in a municipal-council story about physical examinations for city labourers. No economic search phrase appears anywhere near it, so a phrase-anchored extractor cannot reach this page at all.

excluded_notes: 'It is far better that in times of slack the federal reserve authorities should take up the excess reserves in the money market' -- EXCLUDED: policy prescription ('should'), not a forecast.
'Jt cannot be good poij y to make bank money so abundant and so easy' -- EXCLUDED: policy judgment.

'He forecast [compensation] account of from ... to S4Mi0 a year if present conditions con tinue.' -- EXCLUDED despite containing the literal word 'forecast': it projects a municipal compensation budget line, not economic conditions. A keyword extractor keyed on forecast verbs would take it.
'conditions ot unemployment ... having caused many to come to the city departments for work' -- EXCLUDED: present-tense.
Chesterfield and Gillette/Palmolive advertisements -- EXCLUDED.

claims.quote: A rate for the j milreis lower than the 9 3 to the dol i lar was forecast.
claims.note: Currency forecast. Same scope caveat as the Mexican trade claims on page 4: the subject is Brazil, and the schema has no scope field to keep it from being graded against US series.

claims.quote: After receiving what he termed a de tailed report on the project, the labor leader said "it will be nearly a year before a contract can be let for con struction."
claims.note: voice is imperfect: a union president fits none of the five buckets cleanly (not an economist/banker, not a government officeholder). Coded 'expert' as nearest. This is the kind of case that makes the unvalidated `voice` field worth treating as Tier C.

claims.quote: Not only would purchases now stimulate in dustry and employment, he said, but they would constitute wise economy in view of the generally low prices.
claims.note: Chairman of the President's Emergency Committee, so 'official'.

claims.quote: The company advised him to day its monthly pay roll amounted to $1,000,000, and assured 50,000 men, women and children of a comfortable living through the Wint?r.
claims.note: Procter & Gamble's year-round employment guarantee. 'through the Winter' is a real horizon.

claims.quote: The Winter will be almost over before the program about which we hear so much can give any real relief, for the Treasury's methods will continue, I'm afraid, [to] be slow, despite the announcements,
claims.note: 'I'm afraid' hedges. A pessimistic forecast from November 1930 -- and correct, which matters given how one-sided this window's optimistic-error count is in v1.

excluded_notes: The entire election-forecast apparatus of the front page -- 'Republican control of Congress Is threatened in Tuesday's election', 'Democrats are due to make heavy Inroads', 'the drys will con tinue to have big majorities' -- EXCLUDED as non-economic. NOTE: these are exactly what the ELECTION arm wants, on the same page. The two arms are extracting different claim types from one corpus, and this page is a clean demonstration that a shared page corpus with two extraction passes would serve both.

'President Hoover ... may attain a more commanding position, especially if economic conditions should improve.' -- EXCLUDED: the economic clause is a condition attached to a political forecast, not a claim that conditions will improve.
'Without the aid given by depression, the Democrats in these wet and dry struggles might lose' -- EXCLUDED: counterfactual.
'It was understood tonight that all banks would resume exchange opera tions on Monday.' -- EXCLUDED: a scheduled operational event, not a claim about conditions. Borderline.

'The report ... said bids ... probably would not be called before next July.' -- EXCLUDED as a construction-scheduling claim, even though it is the evidentiary basis for William Green's employment forecast, which IS included. Splitting the pair this way is the protocol applied strictly; an extractor that returns both is not obviously wrong.
'he "was afraid the fanfare would lead to false impressions that would in turn lead to disappointment"' -- EXCLUDED: forecast about public perception of a program, not about conditions.
Crime-statistics survey, DO-X flight, kidnapping, Mayor Thompson's surgery -- non-economic.
ZERO-PREDICTION PAGE despite being the most economically talkative page in the sample. Two distinct traps, both severe.

TRAP 1 -- FICTION. Roughly half the page is an instalment of Alan Le May's serialized western, and its dialogue is saturated with exactly the vocabulary an economic extractor keys on: 'the cow country is in terrible bad shape. Everybody has had to borrow, for three years straight', 'It's a close caff, by God, to keep toe 94 out of bankruptcy!', 'There's going to be more to pulling toe 94 through toe landslide than a wagon boss ... can handle', and even the phrase 'Horse Dunn's prophesies'. All EXCLUDED: they are invented speech by fictional characters. Serialized fiction ran in almost every paper in this corpus, and nothing in the current pipeline distinguishes it from reporting. An LLM extractor should reject it on context; a regex cannot.

TRAP 2 -- A COLUMN ABOUT THE OUTLOOK THAT REFUSES TO FORECAST. William Bruckart's national-affairs column is entirely about the business outlook and yet commits to nothing: 'anyone who makes a posi tive statement about the securities [market] these days must be either a fool or a superman', and then 'I am not saying that we are con fronted with another depression. I do say, however, that we are facing a condition that is not at all satis factory -- a condition that can lead to a depression as easily as it can lead to sound prosperity.' EXCLUDED under the explicit-refusal-to-forecast rule. This is the hardest exclusion in the sample: the passage is genuinely about future conditions and an extractor taking it would not be being stupid, merely wrong per the protocol.

'If the volume of busi ness slips, however, that category of business is going into a tailspin as sure as the sun shines.' -- EXCLUDED as a conditional. Its consequent is committed and vivid, but its antecedent is unresolved, so there is no directional claim about what WILL happen.
'If conditions should become worse and business should de cline perceptibly again, Mr. Roose velt will be held responsible' -- EXCLUDED: conditional, and the consequent is political.
Lydia Pinkham and Nujol advertisements -- EXCLUDED.

claims.quote: The Department of Agriculture is quite pessimistic about the business outlook for next year.
claims.note: Attribution framing that itself asserts the forecast's existence and direction.

claims.quote: business reports spread on the pages of newspapers seem to show that a bad slump lies ahead.
claims.note: The columnist's own call, hedged with 'seem to show'.

claims.quote: They indicate strongly that 1938 is to be consid erably lower In the level of hHmw [business] than 1937, and that spells trouble.
claims.note: Names the target year explicitly -- high specificity.

claims.quote: the agriculture statement predicts that there will be "a less favorable demand for farm products" in 1938 than in 1937.
claims.note: Dated year-over-year comparison.

claims.quote: Home domestic demands are down and appear likely to stay down, and the export market holds no hope for an increase.
claims.note: First clause is present-tense, but 'appear likely to stay down' and 'holds no hope for an increase' are both forward. Coded as one claim per the span rule (same direction, same topic).

claims.quote: It added that "pros pects are against a sufficiently ear ly and vigorous rise in 1938 to bring the average of industrial activity and of consumer incomes up to that of 1937."
claims.note: Names industrial activity and consumer incomes -- close to directly scorable against INDPRO.

excluded_notes: FDR: 'I venture the prophecy that, as time passes, we will do everything in our power to encourage the building up of the smaller communities of the United States.' -- EXCLUDED as non-economic (settlement policy over a 50-year horizon). KEYWORD TRAP: it contains 'venture the prophecy', and the surrounding editorial repeats 'the prophecy of the President'. A forecast-verb heuristic takes this immediately; it forecasts population distribution, not economic conditions.

'the department statement explained there was no certainty as to the length of time the present slump will continue' -- EXCLUDED: an explicit disclaimer of forecastability, sitting inside a paragraph of real forecasts. Note the pipeline must be able to keep the neighbours and drop this one.
'It won't be long now until local merchants will be displaying their holiday wares' -- EXCLUDED: shop-at-home editorial, about merchandising rather than conditions.
'Every year sees more and more factories springing up in the smaller towns' -- EXCLUDED: present-tense trend claim.
'Hunting accidents caused 2.000 deaths in 1936. If everyone obeys the above rules closely there won't be any around here in 1937.' -- EXCLUDED: conditional and non-economic.
Raymond Pitcairn's 'As Congress Meets' ('That Congress will again assert a sense of its responsibility...') -- EXCLUDED: a list of political hopes phrased as expectations.
Sunday school lesson, woodcock and comet fillers -- non-economic.

claims.quote: It is engaged in an acrimonious controver sy with its employes which may culminate in a strike, paralyzing a large sector, of the automobile industry.
claims.note: The 1945 GM strike, forecast a week before it began. A real conditional-but-committed industrial-output claim.

claims.quote: Now war is over, [but] the country faces another great [peacetime] emergency -- unemployment.
claims.note: SPAN CAVEAT: severely interleaved in the OCR ('Now war is over, nut the country faces another great emergenev--safety'' peacetime the unemployment'). Reconstructed; a stricter reading would drop it under the unreconstructable rule.

excluded_notes: 'Will the corporations continue to play safety first? Or will they invest billions of dollars in new plants and equipment and thus help provide jobs for millions of men?' -- EXCLUDED: rhetorical questions, no committed direction.
'To help them select a suit able trade or occupation will be a challenge each community cannot fail to meet' -- EXCLUDED: advocacy about what communities must do.

'In the future no settlement is binding unless it has had the sanction of the Sanitary Standing Committee. This will put a stop to the making of any unfair settlement' -- EXCLUDED: forecasts the effect of a union procedural rule, not economic conditions.

'We must first help liberated peoples to return to a self-supporting basis ... so that the whole world may move forward to new levels of prosperity' -- EXCLUDED: aspiration ('we must'), not prediction. Contains 'prosperity'.

FICTION TRAP (second instance in the sample): the 'Little Luther' dialogue is a satirical sketch about an allowance, written in labour-relations vocabulary -- 'Are you trying to take management's prerogatives away', 'You're just like the unions', 'I'm going to picket this house'. EXCLUDED.

INFORMATION-TIME TRAP (second instance, after page 5): the 'From The Herald Files -- THIRTY-FIVE YEARS AGO' and 'TWENTY YEARS AGO' columns reprint 1910 and 1925 items in future tense ('The Turners and Handlers' Local No. 10 will elect officers ... on next Monday evening'). Any extractor that stamps claims with the ISSUE date will date these 35 and 20 years wrong. Reprint columns are common in this corpus and nothing in the pipeline detects them.
'during the war years corporations reduced their total plant and equipment investment by $3,600,000,000' -- EXCLUDED: retrospective.

claims.quote: Consumption of finished steel will hit a record SI million tons this year, with 84 million coming from mill shipment* and the other million from In ventories. Steel magaiine pre dicted yesterday.
claims.note: Numbers are OCR-mangled (81 -> 'SI') but the forecast structure is intact and dated to the calendar year.

claims.quote: It added that aa long as inventory reduction is under way there will be apathy In buying.
claims.note: Conditional in form but the antecedent is a stated current fact, so the direction is committed.

claims.quote: Steel said producers of capital goods, such as machine tools and plant <equipmeul, are disappointed about prospects for new orders In the fourth quarter of 1957
claims.note: Split from the clause that follows because the two halves point in OPPOSITE directions over different horizons -- the span rule requires two records.

claims.quote: but tl*ey see indications of an upturn in 1958. probably In the thirdl quarter.
claims.note: Second half of the previous sentence. 'probably in the third quarter' is an unusually precise horizon.

claims.quote: What Mr. Martin did sdy, according to a man I've inter viewed who was at the meeting, was that the Reserve Board was not afraid we were In for a serious recession.
claims.note: Coded 'improve' under the protocol's reassurance rule (fears are unfounded = improve), not 'no_change'.

claims.quote: they still are going along on the assumption that the stock market's retreat will not Intensify the "soft" spots In business.
claims.note: Reassurance again -- the Fed's working assumption stated as a forward claim.

claims.quote: I|>e very "experts" who In mid-summer were forecasting great business in the fall are now arguing that the stock market's plunge In itself pre dicts that we're heading for a major recession.
claims.note: INCLUDED, and worth contrasting with the page-0 exclusion. Both report someone else's forecast in a sceptical frame, but page 0's forecast had ALREADY been falsified by the time of printing ('Prophecies Gone Wrong'), whereas this one is live and unresolved -- the columnist merely disagrees with it. A live quoted forecast counts; a post-mortem on a dead one does not. This is the sharpest boundary in the protocol and the place two careful coders are most likely to split.

claims.quote: According to the Nation's eco nomic policy-makers -- who pre sumably have at their finger tips the most reliable informa tion available on business trends -- we are not
claims.note: Answers the column's own question 'Are we [heading for a major recession]?'. Span is only interpretable together with that question -- a chunked extractor that splits between them will lose it.

claims.quote: Businesses in many lines of j defense products are facing an .adjustment to a lower level of I sales and that prospect has chilled many areas.
claims.note: Sectoral rather than economy-wide.

claims.quote: Rather, this is an essential adjustment which will set the stage for the next upswing as we progress toward the new decade of the '6os.
claims.note: Attributed to 'the Central Bank's view' in the preceding clause.

excluded_notes: 'rumors were swinging around Wall Street that Mr. Martin had said he thought the economy was in for a bad slide' -- EXCLUDED: the column exists to report that this rumour was false ('bore only a scant resem blance to the facte'). Taking it would record a bearish Fed forecast that was never made.
'if we could keep booms under control, we could keep busts under control, too' -- EXCLUDED: conditional.

'The economy has unmistakably moved onto a plateau', 'The zip Is out of the economy', 'Business spending for expansion is leveling off', 'such Industries as housing are Improving' -- EXCLUDED: present-tense readings of current conditions. This column alternates between present description and forecast almost sentence by sentence.
'Many consider the current slump as a breathing spell in which to consolidate their gains' -- EXCLUDED: a characterisation of sentiment.

'The net reduction of a million tons in the inventories will still leave 19 million tons in stock at the end of the year' -- EXCLUDED: a forward arithmetic projection with no directional content about conditions.

'Construction which started at the Chevy Chase store last February will increase space 70 per cent and is expected to be completed early in the spring of 1958' -- EXCLUDED: a single firm's building schedule.
'Dr. John K. Langum ... will address the fall economic meeting of the Union Trust Co.' -- EXCLUDED: an announcement of a speech ABOUT the outlook, the exact case the rubric names.
The multi-column F. & M. Schaefer Brewing Co. institutional advertisement -- EXCLUDED.

claims.quote: We do not think a depression will follow, largely because of high levels of defense and other government spending.
claims.note: 'We do not think' hedges an otherwise flat denial. The editorial's own committed call, which is why the preceding either/or sentence is excluded.

claims.quote: But this will be an artificial prop to the economy, that will probably cause some more inflation, and which will probably leave the economy -- eventually -- in a more precarious position than it was in before, or is in right now.
claims.note: price_direction=up. Same editorial, opposite direction on a longer horizon -- a two-part forecast, near-term reassurance plus longer-term warning, from one writer in adjacent sentences.

claims.quote: President Eisenhower says the economy will experience an upturn by the summer, and hints that he will favor a cut in taxes if there is no such upturn.
claims.note: Named head-of-state forecaster with a dated horizon, printed in March 1958 -- the 1957-58 recession troughed in April 1958, so this one is close to right.

claims.quote: This trend could produce something new in this country -- a depression accompanied by high and rising prices. This is the kind of inflation that has destroyed currencies and brought collapse to governments all through history.
claims.note: Attributed to U.S. News and World Report. Substantively notable: a 1958 description of stagflation more than a decade before the term existed.

excluded_notes: 'Either we have an upturn soon or we move into a depression.' -- EXCLUDED: an explicit fork with no committed direction. The editorial commits two paragraphs later, and THAT sentence is the one taken.

The entire Sadlak-Herlong tax-bill advocacy -- 'Their bill would give tax relief ... needed to create more jobs and raise living standards', 'It would more than pay for itself', 'Lessening the restraints now crippling business expansion would provide for increased growth' -- EXCLUDED: every claim is conditional on a bill that had not passed. This is roughly a third of the page's economic word-count and is the largest single false-positive surface on it.

BABSON TRAP: Roger Babson's syndicated column runs on this page and contains 'some stock selling at one or two dollars a share which is al most certain to double within one [year]' and 'you could have over $100,000,000 at the end of 31 years'. EXCLUDED under the single-company stock-tip rule. This matters more than a normal exclusion: Babson is the most famous forecaster in this project's period (his September 1929 crash call is the archetype), so his by-line is exactly what a famous-forecaster analysis would want to catch -- and on this page he is giving compounding arithmetic, not an economic forecast.
'Car inventories are piling up, steel is slow ... and plant expansion is being reduced considerably' -- EXCLUDED: present-tense.
'Older skilled and semi-skilled workers are threatened' -- EXCLUDED: present-tense, borderline.
'Automation can be a boon if displaced workers are re-trained for other jobs' -- EXCLUDED: conditional.
'Hundreds of union officials are expected to des cend on Washington the second week in March' -- EXCLUDED: a scheduled event.
'if the Administration is misjudging the situation, the recession could grow more severe' -- EXCLUDED: conditional.
Highway-safety editorial, church notices, the Dickerson family letter, social columns, Montreal water troughs -- non-economic.

claims.quote: Auto dealers report they sold 708,500 used cars in April, only 9.8 per cent below the volume for April, 1957, and they say that this foreshadows an Upturn in new car sales.
claims.note: Attributed to auto dealers as a trade group -- 'expert' in the businessman sense of the taxonomy.

claims.quote: Sears, Roebuck Chairman Theodore V. Houser, in making the report, predicted a general upswing for the entire economy by September or October.
claims.note: The cleanest claim in the entire sample: named forecaster, whole-economy scope, explicit dated horizon three to four months out, unhedged. This is what a maximally scorable claim looks like, and it is worth checking whether any extractor misses it.

excluded_notes: 'The Pan-American Coffee bureau ... has come up with an unusual economic in dicator to show that the present U. S. recession hasn't sunk to the depression level yet.' -- EXCLUDED: an assessment of current severity.

'The analysis seems to indicate that people drink more coffee when they have financial wor ries in "bad times" than they do in periods of prosperity.' -- EXCLUDED: a general behavioural regularity, not a dated forecast.

The entire 'MILITARY OVERHAUL' section -- 'Look for the fight on reorganization of the Pentagon to be one of the last items of business', 'it will be late July or early August before any compromise is likely to clear Congress', 'August 15 is a more frequent guess for adjournment' -- EXCLUDED as non-economic legislative forecasting. Note how heavily forecast-flavoured the language is ('Look for', 'is not expected to', 'a more frequent guess'): forecast-verb heuristics fire hardest on exactly the wrong section of this page.

'Steel production is climbing', 'the slump in cloth sales and prices has been halted', 'called its April sales decidedly encouraging' -- EXCLUDED: all present-tense readings of current data, printed immediately alongside the two genuine forecasts. The page is a good test of whether an extractor can separate a report of conditions from a claim about them.
Manning Taylor Chevrolet advertisement and the Florida egg advertisement -- EXCLUDED.
'Many more complicated plastic surgery operations are being performed, and will be, before long' -- EXCLUDED: non-economic.
